player_archetypes.4:
Pick 4 faces genuine debate between three distinct archetypes:
(1) an elite, explosive RB with pass-catching ability (Ward-weapon argument), (2) a blue-chip
edge rusher or twitchy pass-rushing LB (Saleh/Bradley defensive priority), or (3) a bookend
tackle (OL foundation). The strongest consensus across five analyst mocks favors the RB
archetype, but that arguably reflects "mock inertia" - the newer reasoning pieces (NFL.com,
CBS) explicitly argue for EDGE/OL at this slot because of positional value. The Bradley scheme
specifically suggests the EDGE or rangy LB archetype fits best ideologically; the RB archetype
fits best from a Ward-development standpoint. Both are defensible.

_league.how_to_read:
Each team is profiled with archetype-based framing rather than analyst-name predictions. The
question is never "which specific player will they take" but "what type of player are they looking
for, and why does that type fit their organizational profile."
Standard sections per team:
Picks & capital - all R1 picks, total picks, capital tier
Leadership - GM, HC, OC, DC (flags: new hire, tenure)
2025 context - record, identity, key performance signals
QB situation - locked / bridge / rebuilding / unknown
Key 2026 offseason moves - arrivals, departures, trades (net effect)
Scheme identity - what this tells us about player preferences
Roster needs (tiered) - urgent (2026 impact) / secondary / latent (2027+ cliff)
Player archetype sought at R1 - specific physical/stylistic profile
GM behavioral fingerprint - trade tendencies, positional affinity, historical patterns
Key constraints & uncertainty flags - what could break assumptions
Predictability tier - HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
